content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse 1

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Address | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" |
| Need clause | "you-have no need [for anything] to-be-written to-you" | "you have no need for anything to be written to you" | "you do not need anyone to write to you about that" |
| Structure | Retains hyphenated compounds | Standard formal | Restructured for natural speech |

**Resolution:** "brothers" adopted (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek ADELPHOI is grammatically masculine plural; "brothers and sisters" is an interpretive expansion. Formal phrasing from LOGOS adopted for the need clause.

---

## Verse 2

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Verb tense | "so comes" (present) | "comes" (present) | "will come" (future) |
| Adverb | "know accurately" | "know accurately" | "know perfectly well" |

**Resolution:** "comes" retained (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek present tense (ERCHETAI) is preserved; PNEUMA's future "will come" shifts the tense. "Accurately" preferred over "perfectly well" as closer to AKRIBOS.

---

## Verse 3

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "When they-say" | "When they say" | "Just when people are saying" |
| Destruction verb | "stands-upon them" | "comes upon them" | "will crash down on them" |
| Birth image | "just-as the birth-pain [comes upon] the-[woman]-having [child] in womb" | "as the birth pains upon a woman with child" | "like labor pains seizing a pregnant woman" |
| Escape clause | "they-shall-not-at-all escape" | "they shall by no means escape" | "there will be no escape" |

**Resolution:** LOGOS provides the best balance. EMET's "stands-upon" is hyper-literal (EPHISTATAI); "comes upon" conveys the same force naturally. PNEUMA's "crash down" is vivid but adds intensity beyond the Greek. "Birth pains upon a woman with child" adopted. "By no means escape" preserves the emphatic double negative (OU ME).

---

## Verse 4

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Address | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" |
| Verb | "should-overtake you" | "should overtake you" | "will not catch you off guard" |
| Thief clause | "as a-thief" | "as a thief" | "like a thief" |

**Resolution:** "brothers" and "should overtake you as a thief" adopted (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA restructures the sentence and shifts "catch you off guard" to a different idiom. PNEUMA's note acknowledges the Sinaiticus reads KLEPTAS (plural "thieves") but retains singular — all three use singular.

---

## Verse 5

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Idiom | "sons of-light and sons of-day" | "sons of light and sons of the day" | "children of the light, children of the day" |
| Belonging clause | "We-are not of-night nor of-darkness" | "We are not of the night nor of darkness" | "We do not belong to the night or to darkness" |

**Resolution:** "Sons of light and sons of the day" adopted (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebraic idiom HUIOI is "sons," not the gender-neutral "children." PNEUMA's "do not belong to" is an interpretive expansion of the genitive construction; "are not of" is more faithful.

---

## Verse 6

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Sleep | "let-us-not-sleep" | "let us not sleep" | "let us not fall asleep" |
| Others | "as the rest [do]" | "as the rest do" | "like everyone else" |
| Sobriety | "let-us-watch and let-us-be-sober" | "let us watch and be sober" | "let us stay alert and clearheaded" |

**Resolution:** LOGOS phrasing adopted. PNEUMA's "fall asleep" adds nuance not in the Greek (KATHEUDOMEN). "Stay alert and clearheaded" is interpretive paraphrase of GREGOROMEN KAI NEPHOMEN; "watch and be sober" is closer.

---

## Verse 7

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Structure | "the-[ones]-sleeping, by-night they-sleep" | "those who sleep, sleep at night" | "those who sleep do it at night" |
| Drunk clause | "the-[ones]-being-drunk, by-night they-are-drunk" | "those who are drunk, are drunk at night" | "those who get drunk do it at night" |

**Resolution:** LOGOS phrasing adopted. EMET's bracketed constructions are overly literal. PNEUMA's "do it at night" is idiomatic but loses the parallel structure of the Greek.

---

## Verse 8

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Armor participle | "having-put-on" | "having put on" | "wearing" |
| Love genitive | "of-faith and of-love" | "of faith and love" | "faith and love as a breastplate" |
| Helmet | "[as] a-helmet [the] hope of-salvation" | "as a helmet the hope of salvation" | "the hope of salvation as a helmet" |
| Sobriety clause | "let-us-be-sober" | "let us be sober" | "let us keep our heads" |

**Resolution:** LOGOS adopted. "Having put on" preserves the aorist participle (ENDUSAMENOI). PNEUMA's "wearing" shifts to present continuous. "Keep our heads" is idiomatic paraphrase of NEPHOMEN; "be sober" is more precise.

---

## Verse 9

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Conjunction | "because" | "For" | "For" |
| Verb | "did-not-appoint" | "did not appoint" | "did not destine" |
| Purpose | "unto [the] obtaining of-salvation" | "to the obtaining of salvation" | "to receive salvation" |

**Resolution:** "For God did not appoint us to wrath, but to the obtaining of salvation" adopted. "Because" (EMET) renders HOTI but "For" is more natural in English. PNEUMA's "destine" imports nuance beyond the Greek ETHETO. "Obtaining" (PERIPOIESIN) is more precise than "receive" — it implies active acquisition.

---

## Verse 10

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Relative clause | "the-[one]-having-died for us" | "who died for us" | "who died for us" |
| Watch/sleep | "whether we-watch or whether we-sleep" | "whether we watch or whether we sleep" | "whether we are awake or asleep" |
| Live clause | "together with him we-should-live" | "we should live together with him" | "we will live together with him" |

**Resolution:** LOGOS adopted. EMET's participial form "the-[one]-having-died" is overly literal for TOU APOTHANONTOS. PNEUMA's "we will live" (future indicative) differs from the subjunctive ZESOMEN; "we should live" preserves the subjunctive mood.

---

## Verse 11

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "Therefore" | "Therefore" | "So" |
| Build up | "build-up one the other" | "build up one another" | "building each other up" |
| Also clause | "just-as also you-are-doing" | "just as also you are doing" | "just as you are already doing" |

**Resolution:** "Therefore encourage one another and build up one another, just as you are also doing." PNEUMA's "So" is less formal. "Already doing" adds a word (PNEUMA) not in the Greek; "also" renders KAI faithfully.

---

## Verse 12

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Address | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" |
| Verb | "to-know" | "to know" | "to recognize" |
| Leaders | "presiding-over you" | "who are over you" | "who lead you" |
| Instruction | "admonishing you" | "who admonish you" | "who instruct you" |

**Resolution:** "Recognize" (PNEUMA/LOGOS-aligned) adopted over "know" as EIDENAI here carries the sense of acknowledging or respecting, not mere cognitive awareness. "Who are over you" (LOGOS) for PROISTAMENOUS adopted as formally equivalent. "Admonish" (EMET/LOGOS) retained over "instruct" — NOUTHETOUNTAS specifically means to warn or correct, not merely teach.

---

## Verse 13

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Degree | "exceedingly-abundantly" | "exceedingly" | "in the highest regard" |
| Structure | "to-esteem them exceedingly-abundantly in love" | "to esteem them exceedingly in love" | "Hold them in the highest regard, with love" |

**Resolution:** "Esteem them exceedingly in love" adopted (LOGOS). EMET's "exceedingly-abundantly" attempts the triple-compound HYPEREKPERISSOU but is awkward. PNEUMA's "highest regard" is interpretive paraphrase. LOGOS strikes the balance.

---

## Verse 14

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "Now we-exhort you" | "And we exhort you" | "And we urge you" |
| Address | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" |
| Disorderly | "admonish the disorderly" | "admonish the disorderly" | "warn those who are idle" |
| Fainthearted | "encourage the faint-hearted" | "encourage the fainthearted" | "comfort the discouraged" |
| Weak | "hold-fast-to the weak" | "hold fast to the weak" | "support the weak" |
| Patience | "be-long-suffering toward all" | "be patient toward all" | "be patient with everyone" |

**Resolution:** "Disorderly" (EMET/LOGOS) over "idle" (PNEUMA) — ATAKTOUS means "out of rank," not merely "idle." "Fainthearted" (EMET/LOGOS) over "discouraged" — OLIGOPSUCHOUS literally means "small-souled." "Hold fast to" (EMET/LOGOS) over "support" — ANTECHESTHE means to cling to or hold firmly. "Patient" adopted as modern equivalent of "long-suffering."

---

## Verse 15

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Opening | "See that" | "See that" | "Make sure that" |
| Evil clause | "renders evil for evil" | "renders evil for evil" | "pays back wrong for wrong" |
| Good | "pursue the good" | "pursue that which is good" | "pursue what is good" |

**Resolution:** "See that" (EMET/LOGOS) adopted — HORATE is literally "see/look." "Renders evil for evil" preserved (EMET/LOGOS); "pays back wrong for wrong" is dynamic paraphrase. "What is good" adopted as concise middle ground.

---

## Verse 16

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Word order | "Always rejoice" | "Rejoice always" | "Rejoice at all times" |

**Resolution:** "Rejoice always" (LOGOS) — standard English word order. EMET mirrors the Greek word order (PANTOTE CHAIRETE). PNEUMA's "at all times" expands the single adverb.

---

## Verse 17

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Rendering | "Unceasingly pray" | "Pray without ceasing" | "Never stop praying" |

**Resolution:** "Pray without ceasing" (LOGOS) adopted. EMET's "Unceasingly pray" mirrors Greek order but is stilted. PNEUMA's "Never stop praying" is natural but shifts from the adverb ADIALEIPTOS to a negative imperative construction.

---

## Verse 18

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Preposition | "unto you" | "for you" | "from you" |
| Will clause | "this [is the] will of-God in Christ Jesus unto you" | "this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you" | "this is what God wants from you in Christ Jesus" |

**Resolution:** "For you" (LOGOS) adopted for EIS HUMAS. PNEUMA's "from you" and "what God wants" significantly paraphrase; "will of God" is the standard rendering of THELEMA THEOU.

---

## Verse 19

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Verb | "do-not-quench" | "Do not quench" | "Do not stifle" |
| Word order | "The Spirit do-not-quench" | "Do not quench the Spirit" | "Do not stifle the Spirit" |

**Resolution:** "Do not quench the Spirit" (LOGOS). "Quench" (SBENNUTE) is the literal rendering of extinguishing fire. PNEUMA's "stifle" is a viable dynamic equivalent but loses the fire metaphor. Standard English word order adopted over EMET's Greek-mirrored order.

---

## Verse 20

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Verb | "do-not-despise" | "Do not despise" | "Do not dismiss" |
| Word order | "Prophecies do-not-despise" | "Do not despise prophecies" | "Do not dismiss prophecies" |

**Resolution:** "Do not despise prophecies" (LOGOS). "Despise" (EXOUTHENEITE) means to treat with contempt or count as nothing — stronger than "dismiss." Standard word order adopted.

---

## Verse 21

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| All things | "test all-things" | "test all things" | "test everything" |
| Hold fast | "hold-fast the good" | "hold fast that which is good" | "hold on to what is good" |

**Resolution:** "Test all things; hold fast what is good." Minor wording differences only. "Hold fast" (KATECHETE) preferred over PNEUMA's softer "hold on to."

---

## Verse 22

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Verb position | "From every form of-evil abstain" | "Abstain from every form of evil" | "Stay away from every form of evil" |

**Resolution:** "Abstain from every form of evil" (LOGOS). All three agree on "form" (EIDOUS) over "appearance." PNEUMA's "Stay away from" is more colloquial than APECHESTHE warrants. Standard word order over EMET's Greek-mirrored order.

---

## Verse 23

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Sanctify | "sanctify you wholly" | "sanctify you wholly" | "make you completely holy" |
| Preservation | "be-kept blamelessly" | "be preserved complete, without blame" | "be kept blameless" |
| Tripartite frame | "your whole [being] — the spirit and the soul and the body" | "your spirit and soul and body" | "your whole being, spirit, soul, and body" |

**Resolution:** Composite rendering adopted. "Sanctify you wholly" (EMET/LOGOS) over "make you completely holy" — HAGIASAI is specifically "sanctify." "Your whole being — spirit and soul and body — be kept blameless" combines EMET's framing device with PNEUMA's concise "blameless" (AMEMPTOS). LOGOS's "preserved complete, without blame" splits HOLOTELEIS and AMEMPTOS into separate phrases — valid but wordier.

---

## Verse 24

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Subject | "Faithful [is] the-[one]-calling you" | "Faithful is he who calls you" | "The one who calls you is faithful" |
| Completion | "who also will-do [it]" | "who also will do it" | "and he will do it" |

**Resolution:** "Faithful is he who calls you, who also will do it" (LOGOS). The predicate-first construction ("Faithful is...") mirrors the Greek emphasis. PNEUMA's restructuring loses this emphasis.

---

## Verse 25

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Address | "Brothers" | "Brothers" | "Brothers and sisters" |
| Preposition | "pray also concerning us" | "pray also for us" | "pray for us as well" |

**Resolution:** "Brothers, pray also for us" (LOGOS). "For us" is natural English for PERI HEMON in this context. PNEUMA's "as well" repositions KAI — "also" before the verb is closer to the Greek.

---

## Verse 26

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Word order | "Greet the brothers all" | "Greet all the brothers" | "Greet all the brothers and sisters" |
| Inclusivity | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" |

**Resolution:** "Greet all the brothers with a holy kiss" (LOGOS). Standard English word order. ADELPHOUS is masculine plural.

---

## Verse 27

| Aspect | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA |
|--------|------|-------|--------|
| Adjuration | "I-adjure you [by] the Lord" | "I adjure you by the Lord" | "I put you under oath before the Lord" |
| Command | "[that] the letter be-read to-all the brothers" | "that this letter be read to all the brothers" | "have this letter read to all the brothers and sisters" |

**Resolution:** "I adjure you by the Lord" (EMET/LOGOS) — concise and solemn. PNEUMA's "put you under oath before the Lord" is a valid paraphrase of ENORKIZO but longer. "This letter" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) added for clarity — the Greek TEN EPISTOLEN benefits from the demonstrative in English.

---

## Verse 28

No meaningful disagreements. All three render essentially the same closing benediction. Minor: EMET supplies "[be]" in brackets; LOGOS and PNEUMA state it without brackets. All three omit "Amen" following the Sinaiticus reading.

---

## Summary of Recurring Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Notes |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| PNEUMA uses "brothers and sisters" for ADELPHOI | vv. 1, 4, 12, 14, 25, 26, 27 | Consensus retains "brothers" as formal equivalent |
| EMET mirrors Greek word order | vv. 16, 17, 19, 20, 22, 24 | Consensus uses standard English order |
| PNEUMA paraphrases verbs dynamically | vv. 6, 8, 9, 14, 15, 19, 20 | Consensus prefers formal equivalents |
| EMET uses hyphenated compounds | Throughout | Consensus follows standard spacing |
| Tense disagreements | vv. 2, 3, 10 | Consensus preserves Greek tense where possible |

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, reviewing EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA translations against Codex Sinaiticus, page 203.*
